Heather Irwin

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-07-060
Dear Ms. Irwin:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the lobbyist
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ,.Act,,).r
QUESTION
Is the State Bar of California (the "state Bar') a lobbyist employer subject to
registration under the Act?
CONCLUSION
No' Because the State Bar's lobbyist is an ernployee who is designated in its
conflict of interest code, he falls under the exemption in Section 86300, and the State Bar
does not have reporting obligations.
FACTS
You are an attorney with the State Bar's Office of General Counsel. The State
Bar is a public corporation established in the judicial branch of government for the
pu{pose of performing governmental functions in the administration ofjustice. (Cal.
Const., art. VI, $ 9; Bus. & Prof. Code $ 6001.) In matters of admission and discipline of
attorneys, the State Bar acts as an administrative arm of the Califomia Supreme Court
and is subject to the immediate and plenary review of the high court. In 1985, the State
Bar received an advisory letter from the General Counsel of the Fair Political practices
Commission (the "Commission") stating that, because the State Bar is a "state agency,,
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, both the State Bar and its in-house
lobbyist are exempt from reporting and registration obligations that Chapter 6 of the Act
imposes.
' Govern*"rrt Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle 2,sections
18109-18997, ofthe California Code of Regulations.
t::


File No. 4-07-060
Page No. 2
In 7997, the Commission clarified the State Bar's reporting requirements in the
Goldman Advice letter, I-97-532, stating that the exclusion for employees of the State of
California sometimes applies to consultants or outside counsel hired by a state agency.
The letter also stated that a consultant meets the definition of an "employee of the state of
California" for purposes of Section 86300 if the consultant is covered by the state
agency's conflict of interest code. In 1997, the State Bar qualified as a lobbyist
employer.
You have written to us again as the State Bar has recently changed its practices.
Currently, the State Bar does not use any outside lobbyists. In February of this year,
Anthony Williams, the Director of Governmental Affairs, became a parttime employee
with the State Bar. In his non-State Bar time, he engages in lobbying for other entities.
He has registered as a lobbyist and as part of a lobbying firm.
under the Act, a lobbyist ,rr,il1:lir'i,r". accounting and reporting
obligations. (Sections 86116, 86116.5; Regulations 18615, l86l6.i a lobbyist "nploy".
includes anyperson, other than a lobbying firm, who employs one ormore lobblrtr fo.
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action. (Section 82039.j.) The
Act, however, exernpts from the lobblst provisions of the Act "employees of the State of
California actingwithin the scope of [their] employment.,, (Section g6300(a).)
Lobbyists who work for state agencies are still subject to the ten dollar gift iimitation,
however. (Ibid.)
As stated above, for the purposes of the Act, this office considers the State Bar a
"state age,ncy." Therefore, if the State Bar employs a lobbyist who is designated in the
State Bar's conflicts of interest code, he or she is exanpt from the lobbyisiprovisions of
the Act. As we have previously advised, if an employee subject to the exemption in
Section 86300 is already a registered lobbyist, he or she is not required to refort
payments received from a state agency for lobbying services. (See Quessenberry Advice
Letter, A-84-l2l (copy enclosed).) For these reasons, at this time the State Bar does not
have reporting obligations under the Act.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

Un{er the reasoning of the Billy Advice lefter, it appears that Mr. Williams is now an
"individual contract lobbyist." However, he is stili an ernployee of the State Bar in all
other respects, rather lh*,T independent contracror. As iuc[, ne is siit] a..ignut"a in

Therefore, lt appears that the State Bar is currently exempt from the lobbyist emptoyer

Thank you for your consideration. you can contact me at (415) 53g-2029. I look

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is the State Bar of California (the "state Bar') a lobbyist employer subject to
registration under the Act?

CONCLUSION: No' Because the State Bar's lobbyist is an ernployee who is designated in its
conflict of interest code, he falls under the exemption in Section 86300, and the State Bar
does not have reporting obligations.
